name: Sennacherib and Hezekiah
notes: The Assyrian invasion under Sennacherib, Rabshakeh's blasphemous siege-speech, Hezekiah's prayer, the LORD's deliverance by the angel, Hezekiah's sickness and the sign of the shadow, and the Babylonian envoys, told nearly word-for-word in 2 Kings 18-20 and Isaiah 36-39. This is the Old Testament's most sustained near-verbatim parallel: long stretches match clause for clause, so most links carry the shared KJV wording. Each book also keeps unique material that reads in place and is left unlinked: Kings preserves Hezekiah's tribute and the stripping of the temple gold (2Kgs 18:14-16), Isaiah preserves Hezekiah's own writing or psalm of thanksgiving after recovery (Isa 38:9-20), and 2 Chronicles 32 gives a compressed summary that adds Hezekiah's water-works and defensive preparations. Small differences of arrangement and wording, such as Isaiah placing the figs-and-boil and the sign-question at the close of chapter 38 where Kings keeps them within the healing account, are complementary vantages on one event and are left as a matter for further study. The Chronicles verses are attached to their Kings and Isaiah counterparts at each shared beat.
